item.title: I Hired a Private Investigator to Track Down This Insane 12k-Mile 1970 Lincoln Continental Mark III
item.description: When rumors of a 12,000 mile survivor Lincoln Mark III surfaced on the Internet, Dave jumped into action to find the car.
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.description: How to resolve IISExpress port conflicts in ASP.NET development in 2026 and the age of .NET Core, Docker, and WSL.

item.title: GitHub Actions Is Slowly Killing Your Engineering Team - Ian Duncan
item.description: Why GitHub Actions is the Internet Explorer of CI, and why Buildkite offers a better path forward for teams that care about developer experience.

item.title: Working on products people hate
item.description: I’ve worked on a lot of unpopular products.

item.title: Make MacOS 26 consistently bad (unironically)
item.description: Alongside the various bugs you get, one of the issues of upgrading to MacOS 26 is that it has one of the most notorious inconsistency issues in windows corners. I'm not sure what exactly pushes product designers to like the excessive roundness11. One of the ugliest roundness examples I've ever seen is the current one in the YouTube UI design. I believe that UI design is the most influential field ever since designers just try to follow whatever big companies do (in fact I see this a lot in my work, when two designers are having an argument, one of them would resolve it to, let's see how apple draw that button), which means that we are probably going to see this ugly effect elsewhere very soon.

item.title: FaceTime with My 19-Year-Old Self
item.description: I have a tool that can write code in any language, build almost any app I can imagine, and it costs next to nothing. This would have been wishes 1, 2, and 3 for my 19-year-old self. So why do I feel a growing sense of dissatisfaction and frustration about it all? I imagined talking to him to find out.
item.author: (David Tran)

item.title: How I work without a mouse
“Good Taste” Is Just Experience
item.description: What people call “good taste” is really just experience earned through reps. Stop making it sound innate.

item.title: LLMs Are Ruining My Craft - Brian Carpio
item.description: Inspired by Alex Tatiyants

item.title: Open Source Gave Me Everything Until I Had Nothing Left to Give
item.description: I thought I was having a spiritual awakening. I was having a psychiatric emergency. I was at a tech conference in Sweden when it started. I hadn't slept in...

item.title: My heuristics are wrong. What now?
item.description: More words. More meaning?

item.title: How I vibe-code 10+ web services from anywhere
item.description: How I use tried and trusted Linux/Unix tools to do software development from anywhere
item.author: (Fredrik Berglund)

item.title: Point and Shoot Testing
item.description: It's an often brought up tool within testing circles. In fact, Playwright themselves advertise the tool with the headline "Playwright enables reliable end-to-end testing for modern web apps". And for a lot of people that get to the point of stability within their web app, I'm sure having end-to-end testing may be useful. In my case, I find myself often iterating on my web app's designs until I get to the place I like them, so a UI test seems like it would be counterintuitive. However, with the age of end-to-end testing frameworks comes the ability to rapidly snapshot the state of your application. And this ability requires no tests or complex state machines to execute on. It's just like the classic camera saying, just "Point and Shoot!". However, with this ability comes an almost unbelievable productivity boost when working on web apps targeting multiple screen sizes. Especially in the age of agents. You might be wondering, what do agents have to do with this? Well, in truth, not so much, because many of these benefits extend to manual programming too. So let's get to it then, what are the benefits? Well, as soon as you make any UI change, you have the ability to run a 2s script and instantly know which UI scenarios changed, across many different screen sizes, instantly. No longer is there the requirement to fumble with browser dev tools to shrink the screen. No longer is there the need to have a phone and tablet to test your changes with. It's now a simple command run, and instantly changes across every single screen size and scenario you care about are shown. This is especially handy when these pictures are put into CI because you can get an easy indicator of which UI scenarios did and didn't change, which before having this seemed like a nice benefit, but after having used this for several design changes, is a lifechanger. Ultimately, you just can't go wrong with more visibility into your app.

item.title: The State of Immutable Linux
item.description: Lies, damn lies, and read only filesystems

item.title: dein.fr - Charles-Axel Dein

item.title: The Hidden Contract in Every API Call
item.description: Web apps are distributed systems. The client’s observed world state should be an explicit input to every API call.

item.description: Incus vs. Kubernetes: Why simplicity wins for cost, ops, and cloud freedom. Discover a pragmatic path beyond microservices complexity.

item.title: on supply chain attacks | ozair.dev
item.description: supply chain attack: a way to execute malicious code on many computers by sneaking in the bad code into a highly used open source library.

item.author: (https://soranews24.com/author/casey-baseel/)

item.title: Compromised telnyx on PyPI: WAV Steganography and Credential Theft
item.description: Analysis of malicious telnyx 4.87.1 and 4.87.2 on PyPI — a package with over 1 million monthly downloads: injected code uses WAV audio steganography to deliver payloads that steal credentials and establish persistence. Attributed to TeamPCP.

item.title: Microsoft insiders push to end mandatory Microsoft Accounts on Windows 11
item.description: Microsoft's big sweeping set of changes coming soon to Windows 11 don't address its controversial Microsoft account requirements, but that might soon change.

item.title: How I Drowned a Bureaucrat before dinner., Sightless Scribbles
item.description: A fabulously gay blind author.

item.title: jsongrep is faster than {jq, jmespath, jsonpath-rust, jql}
item.description: An introduction to the jsongrep tool, a technical explanation of its DFA-based search engine, and performance results against popular JSON query tools.

item.title: Bring back MiniDV with this Raspberry Pi FireWire HAT
item.description: In my last post, I showed you to use FireWire on a Raspberry Pi with a PCI Express IEEE 1394 adapter. Now I'll show you how I'm using a new FireWire HAT and a PiSugar3 Plus battery to make a portable MRU, or 'Memory Recording Unit', to replace tape in older FireWire/i.Link/DV cameras. The alternative is an old used MRU like Sony's HVR-MRC1, which runs around $300 on eBay1.
